questions.questionText: Malignant otitis externa is caused by:
questions.optionB: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (invasive external ear infection in elderly diabetics/immunocompromised — osteomyelitis of temporal bone, cranial nerve palsies)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Malignant OE = Pseudomonas, elderly diabetic, skull base osteomyelitis. Why correct: Malignant (necrotizing) OE: severe infection starting in EAC → spreads to skull base → osteomyelitis of temporal bone → cranial nerve palsies (VII first, then IX, X, XI). Almost exclusively in elderly diabetics or immunosuppressed. Why A wrong: S. aureus — common OE, not the necrotizing form. Why C wrong: Aspergillus — otomycosis (fungal OE: wet, itchy ear, fungal hyphae visible). Why D wrong: Streptococcus — less common in OE. Clinical relevance: Diagnosis: CT temporal bone (bone erosion), Tc-99m bone scan + Ga-67 scan. Treatment: prolonged IV anti-pseudomonal antibiotics (ciprofloxacin), may need surgical debridement. Mortality significant. Exam trap: 'Malignant' is a misnomer — it's an INFECTION, not cancer. Named for aggressive/destructive behavior. Memory aid: 'Malignant OE = Miserable diabetic + Pseudomonas = Menacing skull base infection.' Reference: Dhingra – External Ear.
questions.conceptTag: Malignant OE Pseudomonas diabetic skull base osteomyelitis
